description:
A logistics firm must devise a procurement and storage schedule for a critical fuel additive over a 19‑week operational cycle.  
The starting stock at the cycle’s outset is 93 gallons.  
In each week t = 1,…,19, the supplier permits the firm to place a fuel‑additive order, the size of which must be between 9 and 70 gallons.  
Every order, however, requires a lead time of 1 week before it becomes available to meet requirements or replenish stock.  
Weekly additive requirements are as follows:  
* Week 1: 41 gallons  
* Week 2: 42 gallons  
* Week 3: 50 gallons  
* Week 4: 13 gallons  
* Week 5: 22 gallons  
* Week 6: 31 gallons  
* Week 7: 39 gallons  
* Week 8: 56 gallons  
* Week 9: 21 gallons  
* Week 10: 46 gallons  
* Week 11: 22 gallons  
* Week 12: 23 gallons  
* Week 13: 12 gallons  
* Week 14: 22 gallons  
* Week 15: 17 gallons  
* Week 16: 28 gallons  
* Week 17: 58 gallons  
* Week 18: 30 gallons  
* Week 19: 31 gallons  

Shortfalls are allowed, but any unmet requirement is lost and cannot be recovered later.  
Throughout the cycle, additive quantities may be treated as continuous, and the total volume held in the storage tank at any moment must not surpass the capacity limit of 93 gallons.  

In week 13, the maximum permissible order size is restricted to 60 gallons.  
In week 4, the on‑hand stock must be no less than 72 gallons.  

Total expenditure over the cycle comprises three elements: the procurement cost, charged at 2 per gallon ordered; the storage cost, assessed at 3 per gallon kept from one week to the next; and the shortage penalty, levied at 12 per gallon of unsatisfied requirement. The objective is to identify the optimal order quantity for each week and to monitor the ensuing stock and shortage levels, thereby minimizing the overall expenditure.

description:
A shipping company must devise a replenishment and storage schedule for a critical fuel reserve across a 5-week voyage.  
The initial fuel reserve at the start of the voyage is 26 thousand liters.  
In each week \(t = 1, \dots, 5\), the supplier permits the company to request a fuel delivery whose volume must be between 17 and 50 thousand liters.  
However, each delivery requested will require a 3‑week transit time before it becomes available for consumption or for adding to the reserves.  
The projected fuel consumption for each week is as follows:  
* Week 1: 32 thousand liters  
* Week 2: 37 thousand liters  
* Week 3: 30 thousand liters  
* Week 4: 49 thousand liters  
* Week 5: 50 thousand liters  

Shortfalls are allowed, but any unsatisfied consumption is simply lost and will not be carried forward.  
Throughout the voyage, fuel quantities may be treated as continuous, and the total volume of fuel held in reserve tanks at any moment must not exceed the tank capacity of 57 thousand liters.  

In week 5, the maximum delivery volume allowed is 17 thousand liters.  
In week 1, the on‑hand reserve must be at least 46 thousand liters.  

The total voyage cost comprises three elements: the delivery cost, which is 5 per thousand liters ordered; the storage cost, which is 2 per thousand liters kept from one week to the next; and the shortage penalty, which is 6 per thousand liters of unmet consumption. Determine the optimal delivery volume for each week and monitor the resulting reserve levels and shortfalls so as to minimize the total cost.

The initial fuel reserve in the main depot at the voyage's commencement is 11 kiloliters.  
For each leg \( t = 1, \dots, 5 \), the fuel supplier permits the company to schedule a delivery, the volume of which must be between 19 and 71 kiloliters.  
Nevertheless, any scheduled delivery experiences a lag of 3 days before it reaches the depot and becomes available for consumption or to augment reserves.  
The projected fuel consumption for each leg is as follows:  
* Leg 1: consumption 48 kiloliters  
* Leg 2: consumption 45 kiloliters  
* Leg 3: consumption 47 kiloliters  
* Leg 4: consumption 28 kiloliters  
* Leg 5: consumption 38 kiloliters  

Fuel deficits are tolerated, but any consumption not met from available reserves is simply lost and will not be compensated later.  
Throughout the voyage, fuel quantities may be treated as continuous, and the total volume of fuel physically held in the depot at any instant must not surpass the storage limit of 42 kiloliters.  

Specifically on leg 2, the maximum permissible delivery volume is 71 kiloliters.  
Also on leg 2, the on-site fuel reserve must be maintained at a minimum of 2 kiloliters.  

The aggregate expenditure over the voyage comprises three elements: the procurement cost, amounting to 3 per kiloliter ordered; the storage cost, amounting to 3 per kiloliter of fuel kept from one leg to the next; and the deficit penalty, amounting to 9 per kiloliter of unmet consumption. The objective is to ascertain the optimal delivery volume for each leg and to monitor the consequent reserve and deficit levels so as to minimize the total expenditure.

description:
A shipping company must devise a fuel procurement and storage strategy for a fleet of vessels over a voyage lasting 5 consecutive legs.  
The initial fuel reserve in the main depot at the voyage's commencement is 11 kiloliters.  
For each leg \( t = 1, \dots, 5 \), the fuel supplier permits the company to schedule a delivery, the volume of which must be between 19 and 71 kiloliters.  
Nevertheless, any scheduled delivery experiences a lag of 3 days before it reaches the depot and becomes available for consumption or to augment reserves.  
The projected fuel consumption for each leg is as follows:  
* Leg 1: consumption 48 kiloliters  
* Leg 2: consumption 45 kiloliters  
* Leg 3: consumption 47 kiloliters  
* Leg 4: consumption 28 kiloliters  
* Leg 5: consumption 38 kiloliters  

Fuel deficits are tolerated, but any consumption not met from available reserves is simply lost and will not be compensated later.  
Throughout the voyage, fuel quantities may be treated as continuous, and the total volume of fuel physically held in the depot at any instant must not surpass the storage limit of 42 kiloliters.  

Specifically on leg 2, the maximum permissible delivery volume is 71 kiloliters.  
Also on leg 2, the on-site fuel reserve must be maintained at a minimum of 2 kiloliters.  

The aggregate expenditure over the voyage comprises three elements: the procurement cost, amounting to 3 per kiloliter ordered; the storage cost, amounting to 3 per kiloliter of fuel kept from one leg to the next; and the deficit penalty, amounting to 9 per kiloliter of unmet consumption. The objective is to ascertain the optimal delivery volume for each leg and to monitor the consequent reserve and deficit levels so as to minimize the total expenditure.

description:
A shipping company must devise a fuel procurement and storage strategy for a fleet of vessels over a voyage lasting 5 consecutive legs.  
The initial fuel reserve in the main depot at the voyage's commencement is 11 kiloliters.  
For each leg \( t = 1, \dots, 5 \), the fuel supplier permits the company to schedule a delivery, the volume of which must be between 19 and 71 kiloliters.  
Nevertheless, any scheduled delivery experiences a lag of 3 days before it reaches the depot and becomes available for consumption or to augment reserves.  
The projected fuel consumption for each leg is as follows:  
* Leg 1: consumption 48 kiloliters  
* Leg 2: consumption 45 kiloliters  
* Leg 3: consumption 47 kiloliters  
* Leg 4: consumption 28 kiloliters  
* Leg 5: consumption 38 kiloliters  

Fuel deficits are tolerated, but any consumption not met from available reserves is simply lost and will not be compensated later.  
Throughout the voyage, fuel quantities may be treated as continuous, and the total volume of fuel physically held in the depot at any instant must not surpass the storage limit of 42 kiloliters.  

Specifically on leg 2, the maximum permissible delivery volume is 71 kiloliters.  
Also on leg 2, the on-site fuel reserve must be maintained at a minimum of 2 kiloliters.  

The aggregate expenditure over the voyage comprises three elements: the procurement cost, amounting to 3 per kiloliter ordered; the storage cost, amounting to 3 per kiloliter of fuel kept from one leg to the next; and the deficit penalty, amounting to 9 per kiloliter of unmet consumption. The objective is to ascertain the optimal delivery volume for each leg and to monitor the consequent reserve and deficit levels so as to minimize the total expenditure.

description:
A logistics company needs to coordinate 3 cargo shipments through 3 different ports.

Each shipment must follow a specific route represented as pairs (Port, Handling time), where each pair indicates the port where the cargo must be processed and the time required for handling. The sequence of pairs shows the mandatory order in which ports must be visited.

Shipment details:
* S1 follows the route: (Port A, 9) → (Port C, 8) → (Port B, 2)
* S2 follows the route: (Port B, 9) → (Port C, 6) → (Port A, 9)
* S3 follows the route: (Port A, 2) → (Port B, 10) → (Port C, 7)

Each cargo handling operation must proceed without interruption once started, and each port can only process one shipment at a time. The goal is to determine the processing sequence for all shipments at the ports so that the total completion time for all shipments is minimized.

description:
A logistics company needs to route 3 shipments through 3 different transfer hubs.

Each shipment must pass through the hubs in a specific order, represented as (Hub, Handling time) pairs, where each pair indicates the hub where the shipment must be processed and the time required for handling. The sequence of pairs defines the mandatory order in which the hubs must be visited.

Shipment details:
* S1 follows the route: (H1, 7) → (H3, 1) → (H2, 10)
* S2 follows the route: (H2, 6) → (H3, 2) → (H1, 4)
* S3 follows the route: (H3, 3) → (H1, 9) → (H2, 8)

Once a shipment begins processing at a hub, the handling must continue without interruption until complete. Additionally, each hub can only process one shipment at any given time. The goal is to determine the processing sequence for all shipments at the hubs such that the total time to complete all shipments is minimized.

description:
A logistics company needs to schedule 4 delivery routes through 4 distribution hubs.

Each route consists of a sequence of handling stages represented as (Hub, Duration) pairs,
where each pair specifies the hub where the stage must occur and the time required for handling.
The order of pairs indicates the mandatory sequence in which the handling stages must be completed.

Route details:
* Route 1 requires the sequence: (Hub C, 5) → (Hub B, 5) → (Hub A, 7) → (Hub D, 7)
* Route 2 requires the sequence: (Hub D, 4) → (Hub A, 5) → (Hub C, 6) → (Hub B, 1)
* Route 3 requires the sequence: (Hub A, 8) → (Hub B, 10) → (Hub C, 2) → (Hub D, 3)
* Route 4 requires the sequence: (Hub C, 7) → (Hub B, 8) → (Hub A, 1) → (Hub D, 2)

Each handling stage must run continuously once it begins and cannot be interrupted,
and each distribution hub can only handle one stage at a time.
The objective is to determine the processing order of all stages at the hubs
so that the total completion time for all routes is minimized.

description:
A logistics company must devise a procurement and storage strategy for a crucial fuel additive over a 10-day operational window.  
The starting stock at the dawn of the planning interval is 6 units.  
For each day \( t = 1, \dots, 10 \), the vendor permits the company to submit a fuel‑additive purchase whose volume must be between 12 and 22 units inclusive.  
Every purchase, however, requires a lead time of 1 day before it becomes available to meet requirements or augment stock.  
The daily requirement for the additive is specified as:  
* Day 1: 41 units  
* Day 2: 34 units  
* Day 3: 56 units  
* Day 4: 24 units  
* Day 5: 60 units  
* Day 6: 22 units  
* Day 7: 38 units  
* Day 8: 23 units  
* Day 9: 47 units  
* Day 10: 55 units  

Stock‑outs are tolerated, but any unsatisfied requirement is entirely lost and not carried forward.  
Throughout the 10‑day span, additive quantities may be treated as continuous values, and the physical stock on hand at any moment must not surpass the storage‑tank limit of 43 units.  
The aggregate expenditure comprises three elements: the procurement cost, charged at 6 per unit purchased; the storage cost, levied at 1 per unit held overnight; and a deficiency penalty, assessed at 8 per unit of unfulfilled daily requirement.  
The objective is to ascertain the optimal purchase volume for each day and to monitor the ensuing stock and shortfall levels so as to minimize the total outlay.

A retail distribution center must devise a procurement and storage strategy for a seasonal beverage concentrate over a 16-week promotional window.  
The initial stock at the start of the planning window is 76 liters.  
In each week \( t = 1, \dots, 16 \), the supplier permits the center to submit a procurement request, the volume of which must fall between 1 and 70 liters.  
However, each requested shipment will experience a 3‑week lead time before it becomes available to meet customer requirements or replenish the on‑hand stock.

* Week 1: requirement 27 liters

description:
A shipping company must devise a replenishment and storage schedule for a critical fuel reserve across a 5-week voyage.  
The initial fuel reserve at the start of the voyage is 26 thousand liters.  
In each week \(t = 1, \dots, 5\), the supplier permits the company to request a fuel delivery whose volume must be between 17 and 50 thousand liters.  
However, each delivery requested will require a 3‑week transit time before it becomes available for consumption or for adding to the reserves.  
The projected fuel consumption for each week is as follows:  
* Week 1: 32 thousand liters  
* Week 2: 37 thousand liters  
* Week 3: 30 thousand liters  
* Week 4: 49 thousand liters  
* Week 5: 50 thousand liters  

Shortfalls are allowed, but any unsatisfied consumption is simply lost and will not be carried forward.  
Throughout the voyage, fuel quantities may be treated as continuous, and the total volume of fuel held in reserve tanks at any moment must not exceed the tank capacity of 57 thousand liters.  

In week 5, the maximum delivery volume allowed is 17 thousand liters.  
In week 1, the on‑hand reserve must be at least 46 thousand liters.  

The total voyage cost comprises three elements: the delivery cost, which is 5 per thousand liters ordered; the storage cost, which is 2 per thousand liters kept from one week to the next; and the shortage penalty, which is 6 per thousand liters of unmet consumption. Determine the optimal delivery volume for each week and monitor the resulting reserve levels and shortfalls so as to minimize the total cost.
